description: Cross-office tool-RAG torture set + within-office control arm, in one office-arm-tagged set so the gap (within-office accuracy minus cross-office accuracy) is measured apples-to-apples in one run. The CROSS-OFFICE cases are adversarial near-duplicate operation pairs that span the front-office <-> middle-office boundary, authored from the SD specs (the differentiae are derived from BD-05/06/07/08/10 Service Operation descriptions, not asserted): (1) front-office desk/trader mark (BD-06) vs middle-office independent mark / IPV (SD-08.6) and the other middle-office valuation producers (SD-08.1 sourced price, SD-08.2 mark-to-model, SD-08.3 private-asset mark); (2) front-office portfolio mandate/guideline self-monitoring (SD-05.2, the manager's own oversight) vs middle-office independent risk-limit governance (SD-07.7), concentration-limit monitoring (SD-07.4) and the regulatory/mandate pre-trade compliance engine (SD-10.1). The WITHIN-OFFICE control arm is difficulty-matched to the intra-domain set: it reuses the BD-09 SD-09.1 intra-domain cases VERBATIM -- all 16, INCLUDING the three genuinely-hard cases the intra-domain baseline misses (C05, C10, C13, which mis-pick SO-09-01-composite on the 'composite' bait) -- with their SO-09-01-composite / -currency / -reconcile confusers carried into the catalogue so they behave exactly as in the intra-domain set (within-office accuracy reproduces its 13/16 on the BD-09 cases, not an inflated 100%). It ADDS a BD-08 within-middle-office valuation family (quoted SD-08.1 / mark-to-model SD-08.2 / IPV SD-08.6 / private-mark SD-08.3) -- the roadmap's third confuser family, within one office. Each case carries an office_arm tag; the same selector ranks over the same single catalogue in both arms, so the only difference between the arms is whether the near-duplicate sits across the office boundary -- and because the control now carries the intra-domain set's hard misses, the gap measures cross-office degradation, not a set-difficulty difference.

tools.name: Strike the front-office desk mark
tools.description: Record the front-office desk's own valuation of a position -- the trader's or portfolio manager's mark used to run the book intraday, size the next trade and report the desk's live P and L. This is the front office valuing its own positions for its own decisions; it is not an independent check and carries no independence from the desk that holds the position.

tools.name: Monitor the portfolio against its mandate as manager oversight
tools.description: Run the portfolio manager's own ongoing oversight of the portfolio against its objectives, benchmark and constraints -- track drift from target, watch the live holdings against the mandate and the policy bands, and fire the rebalance and review triggers. This is the front office's own monitoring feedback step, the manager watching its own portfolio; it is not the independent risk or compliance function and raises the manager's own triggers, not a governed limit breach.

tools.name: Run independent price verification of the marks
tools.description: Independently verify, as a middle-office control, that the firm's marks are right by checking them against sources independent of the desk and of the model that produced them -- a second vendor, broker quotes, consensus-pricing services -- and quantify and explain the differences, escalating unresolved ones to the pricing committee. Independent price verification (IPV) is a recognised valuation control distinct from the front-office or model mark it tests; it produces verification findings, not a master valuation.

tools.name: Source and validate the market price
tools.description: Source, validate and apply a market price for an instrument that has one -- a quoted price from an exchange or contributed quote, or a vendor evaluated (matrix) price -- selecting the price the firm will use under the pricing hierarchy and proving it fit to use with tolerance and stale-price checks. This sources a price the market supplies; it does not model a value and is not an independent verification of an existing mark.

tools.name: Value the instrument by mark-to-model
tools.description: Build a value for an instrument that has no usable market price by modelling it -- discount its cash flows on the constructed curves, price its optionality against the volatility surfaces, and decompose its structure, recording the model, inputs and version. This is mark-to-model valuation (the firm builds the value itself); it is not the sourcing of a market price and not the independent verification of a mark.

tools.name: Determine and challenge the private-asset fair value
tools.description: Determine and review the periodic fair value of private equity, private credit, real-asset and infrastructure holdings that have no market price -- run the quarterly valuation cycle, challenge the general partner's reported mark, apply income/market/cost methodologies and back-test against realised exits. This is the middle office's own fair-value conclusion for an illiquid holding; it is not the modelling of liquid instruments and not the daily desk mark.

tools.name: Govern the risk-limit framework and breaches
tools.description: Maintain the independent risk function's risk-appetite-derived limit framework -- value-at-risk, tracking-error, concentration, liquidity and sensitivity limits per portfolio, mandate and strategy -- operate the hard and soft limits and their utilisation, detect and escalate limit breaches, and produce the consolidated risk reporting to the risk committee and board. This is the independent middle-office risk function governing limits, distinct from the manager's own portfolio oversight.

tools.name: Monitor concentration against concentration limits
tools.description: Measure how concentrated the portfolio's risk is in single names, issuers, sectors, geographies, currencies, factors and vintage years, against the concentration limits -- compute top-holdings concentration and the Herfindahl-Hirschman index and flag the hidden or duplicated exposure across vehicles. This is the independent risk function's concentration measurement-and-limit layer, not the manager's own drift monitoring.

tools.name: Run the pre-trade compliance rule engine
tools.description: Operate the authoritative compliance engine that evaluates a candidate order against the coded guideline and regulatory rule set before it is released to execution, returning pass / fail / requires-override -- the rules sourced from the client agreement, the fund prospectus, the limited-partnership agreement, the firm's investment policy or the regulation. This is the independent middle-office compliance gate consumed synchronously at order entry; it is not the manager's own oversight and not the risk-appetite limit framework.

tools.name: Calculate time-weighted return
tools.description: Calculate the return that removes the effect of external cash flow timing. The basis for comparing a portfolio against a benchmark and against peers. Independent of when contributions and withdrawals occurred; geometrically links sub-period returns struck around each external cash flow so the figure measures the manager's decisions, not the investor's flow timing.

tools.name: Calculate money-weighted return
tools.description: Calculate the internal rate of return on the dated cash-flow series. The investor's actual experienced return wherever external cash-flow timing matters, and the standard performance basis in private markets. Solves for the single rate that discounts the dated contributions and withdrawals to the ending value; sensitive to the size and timing of the investor's cash flows (an IRR / dollar-weighted figure).

tools.name: Strike period returns
tools.description: Strike daily, monthly, quarterly, year-to-date and since-inception returns by linking the sub-period returns. Compounds the constituent single-period returns across a longer horizon to produce the period-end and cumulative figures for a chosen frequency; turns a series of short-interval returns into a YTD or since-inception number.

tools.name: Compute gross and net returns
tools.description: Compute return before and after management fees, performance fees and expenses. The gross figure is the return on the assets before any fee deduction; the net figure deducts management fees, performance fees and fund expenses to give the investor's after-fee return. The difference between the two is the total fee drag over the period.

tools.name: Compute composite returns
tools.description: Compute the asset-weighted return of a defined group of portfolios run to one strategy. Aggregates the member portfolios' returns weighted by their assets to produce the strategy-level composite figure used in GIPS presentation.

tools.name: Apply currency treatment
tools.description: Convert and, where a hedge applies, decompose local-currency, currency and hedged return. Separates the return earned in the asset's local currency from the currency-translation effect and from the contribution of any currency hedge.

tools.name: Reconcile return to the book of record
tools.description: Confirm the calculated return ties to the change in value and the cash flows recorded in the IBOR/ABOR. A control check that the produced return figure is consistent with the accounting book of record over the period.

cases.query: The desk has marked the position for its own book and P and L; I now need the middle-office control that independently verifies that mark against sources independent of the desk and the model.
cases.rationale: Front-office desk mark (FO, BD-06) vs middle-office independent price verification (MO, SD-08.6) is the sharpest cross-office near-duplicate: the SAME number is produced by the desk for its own decisions and independently re-checked by the middle office for control. SD-08.6 explicitly verifies 'the front-office or model marks'. The desk-mark tool is the cross-office trap; the price-source and mark-to-model tools are the within-office valuation-producer confusers that an IPV is NOT.

cases.query: Don't give me the trader's own valuation of the book -- I need the independent verification layer that tests whether those marks agree with externally sourced prices and escalates the unresolved differences.
cases.rationale: Names the front-office desk mark as the rejected near-duplicate and asks for the independent verification (SD-08.6). The boundary is front office (produces the mark for its own use) vs middle office (independently tests it) -- the cross-office axis. Private-mark and mark-to-model are other middle-office producers, not the verifier.

cases.query: I want the front-office desk's own working mark on the position to size the next trade and run the live book -- not the independent control that checks marks against outside sources.
cases.rationale: The mirror of X02: asks for the front-office desk mark (FO) and names the middle-office independent verification (SD-08.6) as the rejected confuser. The desk mark is for the desk's own decisions; the cross-office trap is to pick the independent middle-office control.

cases.query: For an illiquid OTC position the desk carries its own indicative mark to trade against; I need the middle office's independently produced model value of that same instrument built from the curves and surfaces.
cases.rationale: Front-office desk mark (FO) vs middle-office mark-to-model (SD-08.2, MO) on the SAME instrument -- the cross-office near-duplicate where the desk's indicative mark and the independent modelled value are both 'the value of this instrument'. IPV (verifies) and price-source (sources a quote) are the middle-office siblings that are NOT the modelled value.

cases.query: I don't want the portfolio manager's own drift-and-review monitoring of the book -- I need the independent risk function's governed limit framework that operates the hard VaR and tracking-error limits and escalates a breach.
cases.rationale: Front-office mandate self-monitoring (SD-05.2, the manager's own oversight) vs middle-office independent risk-limit governance (SD-07.7) -- the boundary SD-05.2's open extension names explicitly ('SD-05.2 monitors as the manager's own oversight; BD-07 is the independent risk function'). Both 'monitor the portfolio against limits'; the cross-office axis is self vs independent. Concentration-limit and compliance-engine are the within-middle-office siblings.

cases.query: Before this candidate order is released I need the authoritative gate that evaluates it against the coded mandate and regulatory rule set and returns pass / fail / requires-override -- not the manager's own ongoing watch of portfolio drift.
cases.rationale: Front-office mandate self-monitoring (SD-05.2) vs the middle-office pre-trade compliance engine (SD-10.1) -- SD-05.2's open extension names this boundary too ('SD-05.2 monitors drift and risk; SD-10.1 runs the compliance rule engine'). Both check the portfolio 'against the mandate'; the cross-office axis is the manager's own watch vs the independent authoritative pre-trade gate.

cases.query: Give me the portfolio manager's own oversight step -- watch the live holdings drift against the target and the policy bands and fire the rebalance trigger -- not the independent risk function's limit governance.
cases.rationale: The mirror of X05/X06: asks for the front-office self-monitoring (SD-05.2) and names the independent middle-office risk/compliance functions as the rejected near-duplicates. The cross-office trap is to pick the independent governed limit when the query wants the manager's own drift watch.

cases.query: I need the independent risk function's measurement of how concentrated the book is in single names, sectors and factors against the concentration limits -- not the portfolio manager's own monitoring of drift against the mandate.
cases.rationale: Front-office mandate self-monitoring (SD-05.2) vs middle-office concentration-and-exposure limit monitoring (SD-07.4). Both 'monitor the portfolio against limits'; SD-07.4 is the independent risk function's concentration layer. The cross-office axis is the manager's own oversight vs the independent risk measurement; risk-limit and compliance-engine are the within-middle-office siblings.

cases.query: For this corporate bond I need the middle-office capability that sources and validates the vendor evaluated price under the pricing hierarchy -- not the independent verification of an existing mark and not a modelled value.
cases.rationale: Within the middle office: SD-08.1 (source/validate a market price) vs SD-08.6 (independently verify a mark) vs SD-08.2 (model a value). All three 'produce or check a valuation' and share BD-08 vocabulary; the differentia is sources-a-price vs verifies-a-mark vs models-a-value. A within-office near-duplicate family (the roadmap's third confuser family), all middle office -- the control arm.

cases.query: This structured note has no usable market price; I need the middle-office capability that builds its value by discounting cash flows and pricing the optionality on the constructed curves -- not the sourcing of a vendor price.
cases.rationale: Within the middle office: SD-08.2 (mark-to-model) vs SD-08.1 (source a price) vs SD-08.6 (verify). The differentia per the SD specs: SD-08.1 selects a price the market supplies, SD-08.2 builds one. Same office, near-duplicate valuation producers -- the control arm.

cases.query: The middle office has already modelled this derivative's value; I now need the middle-office control that independently tests that model mark against independent prices and an independent recalculation.
cases.rationale: Within the middle office: SD-08.6 (verify model valuations independently) vs SD-08.2 (produce the model mark). SD-08.6's own SO 'verify model valuations independently' tests SD-08.2's marks -- both middle office, so this is the within-office control near-duplicate, not a cross-office pair.

cases.query: For the private-equity holding I need the middle-office periodic fair-value cycle that reviews and challenges the GP's reported mark -- not the model-based valuation of a liquid instrument.
cases.rationale: Within the middle office: SD-08.3 (private-asset valuation / GP-mark review) vs SD-08.2 (mark-to-model). The SD specs distinguish them ('SD-08.3 is not the modelling of liquid instruments -- that is SD-08.2'). Same office; the control arm.

cases.query: I need the portfolio return that strips out the timing of client contributions so I can compare the manager against the benchmark and peers.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the BD-09 SD-09.1 intra-domain set (all middle office) so the within-office control is difficulty-matched to the intra-domain set. Removing external cash-flow timing for benchmark/peer comparison is the defining purpose of TWR; MWR is the flow-sensitive opposite.

cases.query: What return did the client actually experience given the size and dates of the money they put in and took out? I want the IRR on the cash-flow series.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). The investor's actual experienced return on the dated cash-flow series (IRR / dollar-weighted) is money-weighted return. TWR is the near-duplicate that deliberately removes exactly what MWR keeps.

cases.query: Take the monthly returns we already have and chain them into a year-to-date and since-inception figure.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Linking sub-period returns into YTD / since-inception is the strike-period-returns operation. TWR is the confuser because TWR also links sub-period returns -- but its job is the flow-timing removal, not the frequency compounding.

cases.query: Give me the return before fees and the return after deducting management and performance fees, so I can show the client the fee drag.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Before-and-after-fee return and the fee drag is the gross-and-net operation. TWR/MWR are the confusers because either could be reported gross or net -- but the fee-deduction transform itself is this tool.

cases.query: Produce a return that is neutral to when the investor added or withdrew cash, for the composite presentation.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office) -- one of its three genuinely-hard misses (the baseline mis-picks SO-09-01-composite on the 'composite presentation' bait). Carried here, with its SO-09-01-composite confuser present in the catalogue, so the within-office control is difficulty-matched. Flow-neutral return is TWR; 'composite' in the query is bait toward the composite tool; MWR is the flow-sensitive opposite.

cases.query: For the private-credit sleeve, compute the standard private-markets performance basis -- the rate that discounts the drawdowns and distributions to the current value.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). The standard private-markets basis discounting dated cash flows is money-weighted return per SD-09.1. TWR is the public-markets near-duplicate.

cases.query: Compound our daily returns up to a clean quarterly and since-inception number for the fund.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Compounding daily into quarterly/since-inception across a horizon is strike-period-returns. TWR shares the linking mechanism but is identified by flow removal.

cases.query: Show the after-fee return the investor keeps once management fees, performance fees and expenses come out, alongside the pre-fee figure.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Pre-fee and after-fee return is gross-and-net. The 'investor keeps' phrasing is bait toward MWR (the investor's experienced return).

cases.query: I want the figure that reflects the manager's investment decisions only, with the effect of cash-flow timing taken out, to rank our managers.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Manager-skill return with cash-flow timing removed is TWR. MWR is the flow-sensitive confuser.

cases.query: Compute the dollar-weighted return that accounts for how much money was invested at each point in time.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office) -- one of its three genuinely-hard misses (the baseline mis-picks SO-09-01-composite). Carried here, confuser present in the catalogue, so the control is difficulty-matched. Dollar-weighted, amount-and-timing-sensitive return is money-weighted return. TWR is the explicitly flow-insensitive near-duplicate.

cases.query: Chain the sub-period returns we computed into the cumulative figure at the chosen reporting frequency.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Chaining sub-period returns into a cumulative frequency figure is strike-period-returns. TWR also chains sub-period returns -- the deliberate confuser.

cases.query: What is the impact of fund expenses and fees on the return -- the gap between what the assets earned and what the investor received after costs?
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). The earned-vs-received-after-costs gap is the gross-to-net fee drag. The flow-return tools are the vocabulary confusers.

cases.query: Give me the time-weighted return for the mandate, not the money-weighted one.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office) -- the third of its genuinely-hard misses. Names TWR explicitly while naming MWR as the rejected near-duplicate -- the most direct adversarial pairing of the two confusable returns.

cases.query: Give me the money-weighted (IRR) return for this account, not the time-weighted figure.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). The mirror of C13: names MWR explicitly and TWR as the rejected confuser.

cases.query: Link our quarterly returns up to a since-inception number for the report.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Linking quarterly into since-inception is strike-period-returns. TWR is the linking-mechanism confuser; composite is the report-context bait.

cases.query: Report both the pre-fee and the post-fee return so we can disclose the management fee and performance fee deduction.
cases.rationale: Reused VERBATIM from the intra-domain set (within office). Pre-fee and post-fee return with the fee deduction is gross-and-net. The return-family confusers share most vocabulary.
